name: The Fallout Wastes
description: The Fallout Wastes - A scorched open wasteland of collapsed civilization. Post-apocalyptic survival.

1.content: The Fallout Wastes is what remains after everything ended. The bombs fell, or the reactors melted, or both — the records disagree and no one alive was there for it. What's left is a scorched open wasteland under a sky that ranges from dust-yellow to rust-orange and is never quite the right color for any time of day. Cities are ruins. Highways are cracked ribbons leading between clusters of survivors who built something new from the wreckage of something old. The land is irradiated in patches — some hot enough to kill in hours, others merely slow and cumulative. Life here is practical, resourceful, and stubbornly persistent. The old world destroyed itself. The new world uses the pieces and does not intend to make the same mistake, though it recognizes it probably will.

2.content: Culture in the Wastes is built on three principles: waste nothing, trust carefully, and keep your sense of humor. Resources are finite and irreplaceable — every scrap of metal, every clean water ration, every working battery represents someone's labor and risk. People share when they can and trade when they must. The social contract is simple: contribute to the settlement or move along. Freeloaders are not punished — they are simply not fed. Humor is dark, constant, and valued as a survival tool equal to clean water. A settlement that can still laugh is a settlement that still functions. The Wastes has no time for self-pity. What happened, happened. What matters is what you do with what's left.

3.content: The old world is remembered in fragments — stories passed down, sometimes accurately, sometimes not. People know there were cities that touched the sky, machines that thought for themselves, and enough food to throw away. They know this because the ruins prove it. They also know the old world ended because the ruins prove that too. The relationship between the Wastes and the old world is complicated: reverence for what was achieved, contempt for how it ended, and a practical interest in anything that still works. Old-world technology is prized not for nostalgia but for function. A working generator is worth more than a library of pre-war history. Knowledge of how to repair one is worth more than the generator.

4.content: The Wastes stretches across what was once a populated region — the outlines of cities, suburbs, and farmland are still visible beneath the dust and radiation damage. The terrain is mostly flat scrubland punctuated by the skeletons of old-world structures: collapsed overpasses, rusted vehicle graveyards, the shells of buildings that lost their windows and their purpose. Water features are rare and precious — a clean creek draws settlement the way gold once drew prospectors. The radiation is patchy and unpredictable: a perfectly safe field may border a hot zone that glows faintly at night. The landscape is harsh, beautiful in a broken way, and completely indifferent to whether anyone survives in it.

5.content: Dusthaven is the largest trading settlement in the Wastes, built inside and around a pre-war shopping mall whose concrete shell provided ready-made walls and a roof. The interior has been converted into a marketplace where scavengers, traders, and settlers exchange goods. Stalls are built from salvaged materials — car doors as countertops, shopping carts as display racks. The upper floors serve as living quarters. The parking structure is a fortified entrance. Dusthaven has running water from a well dug through the mall's basement floor, a working generator that provides power for four hours each evening, and a council of traders who settle disputes with a pragmatism that passes for law. Population fluctuates between two and four hundred depending on trading season.

6.content: Bunker 14 is one of several pre-war emergency shelters in the region. Most are collapsed, flooded, or irradiated. Bunker 14 is intact and inhabited — a community of roughly eighty people who have lived underground for two generations. The bunker provides filtered air, purified water, and sealed growing chambers that produce enough food to survive on, if not to enjoy. The inhabitants are paler than surface dwellers, cautious around open sky, and knowledgeable about pre-war technology to a degree that makes them valuable trading partners. They trade technical expertise and manufactured goods for surface supplies they cannot produce: fresh food, raw materials, and news. Bunker 14's location is not widely known, which is by design.

7.content: The Rust Yard is a vast field of pre-war vehicles — cars, trucks, buses, military transports — stretching across several square miles of flat ground. The vehicles are in various states of decay, stripped by generations of scavengers for usable parts. What remains is still valuable: frame metal, glass, rubber, wiring. The Rust Yard is a working scavenge site with a semi-permanent camp of scavengers who live among the wrecks and defend their claims with the casual territoriality of people who have too much ground to patrol and not enough people to do it. The Yard is also a landmark — visible for miles, useful for navigation, and a reliable meeting point for trade caravans crossing the open Wastes.

8.content: Greensprout Farm is a fortified agricultural settlement built around a clean aquifer. A family operation that grew into a community, it produces the only reliable fresh food in the region: mutated but edible crops, a small herd of adapted livestock, and clean water that is traded at premium rates. The farm is surrounded by walls of stacked vehicles and concrete barriers, not because of raiders — though they are a concern — but because the crops attract wildlife, and wasteland wildlife has teeth. Greensprout's importance to the regional economy cannot be overstated. It is the reason Dusthaven exists as a trade hub. Threatening Greensprout is the fastest way to unite every settlement in the Wastes against you.

9.content: The Hot Zone is a region of concentrated radiation centered on what was once a population center. The blast or meltdown that created it left a crater surrounded by miles of land where Geiger counters climb and exposure is measured in hours rather than days. The Hot Zone is avoided by anyone with sense. It is entered by scavengers who calculate that the pre-war technology buried in the ruins is worth the exposure cost. These runs are planned carefully: routes mapped, exposure times calculated, protective gear assembled from whatever is available. The Hot Zone glows faintly at certain times of night. The glow is not beautiful but it is visible, and it serves as a reminder of what the old world's final argument looked like.

10.content: Clean Creek is a groundwater-fed stream that emerges from limestone formations east of Dusthaven. The water is naturally filtered through rock and tests clean — no radiation, no chemical contamination. This makes it the most strategically important geographic feature in the Wastes. A small settlement has grown around the creek's emergence point, tasked primarily with protecting the water source and managing distribution. Water rights are the closest thing the Wastes has to formal law. Contaminating, hoarding, or diverting Clean Creek's flow is the one offense that every settlement agrees warrants lethal response. The creek is small — it would be unremarkable in the old world. In the Wastes, it is the difference between survival and migration.

11.content: The Overpass Camp sits atop a section of pre-war elevated highway that survived relatively intact. The elevation provides defensibility, visibility, and a breeze that keeps the worst of the dust at lower levels. The camp houses a transient population of traders, travelers, and drifters who use it as a waystation between settlements. Vehicles that once filled the highway have been pushed to the edges to form walls, and tarps stretched between them create sheltered living spaces. The camp has no permanent leadership — whoever has been there longest generally arbitrates disputes, and when they leave, the role transfers to the next. The view from the overpass extends for miles in every direction, making it the best early-warning position in the region.

12.content: The Dead Mall is a collapsed pre-war retail complex on the eastern edge of the Wastes, distinct from the intact mall that became Dusthaven. This one's roof caved in years ago, leaving a maze of half-buried stores open to the sky. Scavengers pick through it regularly but it keeps producing — pre-war goods sealed in back rooms, in basement storage, in vending machines that somehow still hold inventory. The Dead Mall is considered mildly cursed by local superstition, not because anything supernatural occurs there but because injuries happen at a higher rate than at other salvage sites. The structure is unstable, the floors are unreliable, and the remaining walls have opinions about how long they intend to stand.

13.content: Mama Torrez's Cantina sits at the crossroads where the main trade routes intersect, built from a pre-war gas station with additions bolted on over the years. Mama Torrez herself is a broad woman of uncertain age who brews alcohol from whatever ferments, serves food that is better than it has any right to be, and maintains order through sheer force of personality. The cantina is neutral ground — disputes left outside, weapons holstered, everyone's money good. Information flows through the cantina because everyone passes through eventually. Mama Torrez hears everything and shares selectively, based on criteria no one has fully mapped. Earning her trust is a long project. Losing it is instant and permanent.

14.content: Scavenger crews are small teams — typically three to six people — who enter ruins, hot zones, and unexplored areas to recover pre-war technology, materials, and supplies. Crews are organized by skill: a leader who plans routes and manages risk, a tech who identifies valuable salvage, and muscle who handles threats. Good crews survive through preparation and discipline. Bad crews take unnecessary risks and don't last. The scavenger economy is the backbone of the Wastes — everything the settlements use was found by someone, somewhere, at some risk. Crews trade their finds at Dusthaven or directly with settlements. Reputation matters: a crew known for quality salvage and honest dealing gets better prices and better information about where to look next.

15.content: Each major settlement in the Wastes is governed by a council of its most competent members — not elected, not appointed, but emerged through demonstrated ability to keep things running. Council decisions are practical: resource allocation, defense coordination, trade agreements, dispute resolution. Authority is maintained through usefulness rather than force. A council that stops making good decisions is replaced, informally and without ceremony, by people who make better ones. The councils of different settlements cooperate loosely, primarily through trade agreements and mutual defense pacts arranged at Dusthaven. There is no central government, no constitution, and no law beyond what each settlement enforces within its own walls.

16.content: Raiders are groups that take rather than trade — attacking caravans, stealing from settlements, and surviving through violence. They range from desperate individuals driven to robbery by circumstance to organized crews who have chosen predation as a lifestyle. The Wastes does not romanticize raiders. They are a practical threat, managed through vigilance, fortification, and the willingness to shoot back. Most raider groups are short-lived: they attract attention, settlements coordinate a response, and the group disperses or dies. The persistent ones survive by being smart enough to avoid targets that can fight back, which limits their options and eventually their viability. Raiding is the Wastes' version of a dead-end career.

17.content: Trade caravans are the circulatory system of the Wastes, moving goods between settlements along routes that are as much tradition as geography. A caravan typically consists of pack animals or modified vehicles carrying salvage, food, water, medicine, and ammunition. Caravan leaders are among the most respected people in the Wastes — they know the routes, the risks, the settlements' needs, and the current state of raider activity. They are also the primary carriers of news and information. A caravan's arrival at a settlement is an event: people gather for trade, for news, and for the reassurance that the outside world still exists and still functions. Caravan protection is a common employment for solos and small mercenary groups.

18.content: The Hoarders are a loosely connected network of individuals and small groups who collect, preserve, and study pre-war technology. They are not a faction in the military sense — they are obsessives with a shared fixation. Hoarders maintain hidden caches of technology that they trade reluctantly and only for items of equivalent rarity. Their knowledge of pre-war systems is unmatched, making them invaluable consultants for settlements trying to repair generators, water purifiers, or communication equipment. They are eccentric, secretive, and prone to valuing a working circuit board more highly than human relationships. The settlements tolerate their oddities because the alternative — losing access to their expertise — is worse.

19.content: The Greencoats are a volunteer militia that patrols the trade routes between major settlements, providing security for caravans and responding to raider activity. They wear green armbands made from pre-war military fabric — the color has no tactical significance but provides identification. The Greencoats are not soldiers. They are farmers, traders, and scavengers who rotate patrol duty because the alternative is leaving the routes unprotected. Training is minimal but practical. Equipment is whatever the volunteer owns. What they lack in discipline they compensate for with knowledge of the terrain and personal investment in the routes they protect. The Greencoats are funded by a tax on trade goods passing through Dusthaven, which makes them the Wastes' only publicly funded institution.

20.content: Scavengers in the Wastes are resourceful, risk-aware, and perpetually covered in dust from the last ruin they crawled through. They carry tools as often as weapons: pry bars, wire cutters, multimeters salvaged from pre-war electronics kits. A good scavenger reads a ruin the way a tracker reads ground — noting structural integrity, likely salvage locations, and exit routes simultaneously. They are practical to the point of seeming cold, but this is efficiency rather than indifference. Emotional investment in objects is a liability when your job is deciding what's worth carrying. Scavengers trade stories about the strangest things they've found in ruins, and these stories function as both entertainment and intelligence gathering.

21.content: Doctors in the Wastes practice medicine with limited supplies, improvised equipment, and knowledge assembled from pre-war medical texts, practical experience, and trial-and-error. They set bones, treat infections, manage radiation exposure, and perform surgery under conditions that would horrify a pre-war physician. They are among the most respected people in any settlement because their skills are rare and the need is constant. Wasteland doctors are pragmatic about outcomes — they will tell you honestly what they can and cannot fix, and they do not promise. Payment is flexible: trade goods, labor, future favors. A doctor who refuses to treat someone over payment is not a doctor the Wastes tolerates for long.

22.content: Caravan guards are fighters who have chosen steady employment over freelance survival. They travel with trade caravans, providing security against raiders, wildlife, and the general dangers of wasteland travel. Guards are chosen for reliability over raw combat ability — a guard who panics is worse than no guard at all. They carry well-maintained weapons, know the trade routes, and develop a sixth sense for ambush terrain. The work is monotonous punctuated by moments of intense activity, which attracts a specific temperament: patient, alert, and comfortable with long silences. Guards are paid in goods and guaranteed meals, which in the Wastes constitutes a good living.

23.content: Mechanics in the Wastes keep things running — generators, water purifiers, vehicles, weapons, and anything else with moving parts. Their knowledge is a hybrid of pre-war technical manuals, hands-on experience, and creative problem-solving born from never having the right part. A wasteland mechanic can jury-rig a solution from components that were never meant to work together, and they do this daily. They are in constant demand and compensated accordingly. Mechanics tend to be possessive of their tools, which are harder to replace than any piece of equipment they repair. A mechanic's workshop — usually a cluttered corner of whatever settlement they call home — is considered their territory, and entering without invitation is a social error.

24.content: In every settlement, there is someone old enough to remember — if not the old world directly, then stories from someone who did. These elders carry knowledge that is both valuable and painful: what the world was like when water ran from taps, when food was abundant enough to discard, when the sky was blue. They tell these stories carefully, aware that too much nostalgia is corrosive and too little context makes the present feel inevitable rather than the consequence of choices. Elders who remember are consulted on practical matters — where pre-war infrastructure might still be functional, what old-world technology was designed to do — and on philosophical ones: what went wrong, and how to avoid repeating it.

25.content: Raiders are not monsters. They are people who made a choice — sometimes driven by desperation, sometimes by cruelty, sometimes by the simple calculation that taking is easier than trading. They range from lone opportunists to organized crews with territory and hierarchy. The organized ones are the most dangerous because they plan, coordinate, and learn from mistakes. Raiders who survive long enough develop a professional approach: they scout targets, assess risk, and avoid fights they might lose. The Wastes does not try to reform raiders. It defends against them, punishes them when caught, and recognizes that as long as scarcity exists, some people will choose violence over cooperation. This recognition is not forgiveness. It is realism.

26.content: Radiation in the Wastes is measured, managed, and respected. Geiger counters — pre-war devices maintained obsessively — are the primary tool for assessing risk. Radiation exposure is cumulative: small doses over time produce the same effect as large doses all at once, just slower. Symptoms progress from nausea and fatigue through hair loss and bleeding to organ failure and death. Treatment is limited — purging agents can reduce acute exposure, and rest helps the body recover from mild doses, but sustained high exposure is a death sentence that medicine in the Wastes cannot commute. Every settlement tracks radiation levels in its surroundings. Every scavenger knows their lifetime dose. Radiation is the Wastes' invisible geography — a map of danger that overlays the physical terrain.

27.content: Scavenging is the Wastes' primary industry. Pre-war ruins contain technology, materials, and supplies that cannot be manufactured with current capabilities. A successful scavenge requires knowledge of pre-war construction, an eye for what's valuable versus what's heavy, and the physical ability to access collapsed, irradiated, or structurally unsound locations. The best salvage sites are the most dangerous: military installations, hospital basements, research facilities. These have been picked over by generations of scavengers, but they keep producing because pre-war buildings were large and pre-war people stored things in places that take effort to reach. Diminishing returns are real — each generation of scavengers finds less, searches harder, and takes greater risks.

28.content: Clean water is the base currency of the Wastes. Everything else is valued in relation to it: how much water would this item trade for, how much water does this person need, how much water does this journey require. Water comes from underground sources, rainfall collection, and pre-war purification systems that still function. Contaminated water can be treated but the process is slow and the equipment is rare. Water rights — who can draw from which source, how much, and when — are the most formalized rules in the Wastes. Every settlement maintains reserves. Every traveler carries more than they think they need. Dying of thirst in the Wastes is not uncommon, and it is never treated as someone else's problem if it can be prevented.

29.content: Weapons in the Wastes are a mix of pre-war firearms, improvised melee weapons, and jury-rigged devices that function through a combination of engineering and optimism. Ammunition is scarce and expensive — most fighters carry bladed weapons as primary and save firearms for situations where range matters. Pre-war guns are prized for reliability and accuracy but require ammunition that is increasingly difficult to find. Wasteland-manufactured ammunition exists but varies in quality from functional to dangerous. Combat in the Wastes is avoided when possible — injuries are hard to treat, ammunition is hard to replace, and dying over a dispute that trade could have resolved is considered the worst kind of waste.

30.content: Medicine in the Wastes is divided into two categories: legitimate treatment and chems. Legitimate medicine includes antibiotics scavenged from pre-war stocks, herbal remedies developed through post-war experimentation, and surgical techniques that are crude but effective. Chems are a broader category — stimulants, painkillers, and performance enhancers produced from pre-war pharmaceutical stocks or synthesized from available materials. Chems work, which is why people use them. They also carry costs — addiction, organ damage, cognitive impairment — that make them a calculated risk rather than a solution. Doctors in the Wastes prescribe chems when the alternative is worse and counsel against them when it isn't, but the decision is ultimately the individual's.

31.content: Radiation and environmental contamination have produced mutations in the Wastes' population — both human and animal. Most mutations are minor: slightly altered bone structure, unusual coloring, enhanced or diminished senses. Some are significant: physical deformities, unusual growths, or genuinely new capabilities that defy pre-war biological understanding. Mutated individuals face varying levels of acceptance depending on the settlement. Most communities are pragmatic — if you can work, you're welcome regardless of what you look like. Some settlements are less tolerant. Mutated wildlife is more consistently dangerous: larger, more aggressive, and adapted to conditions that would kill their pre-war ancestors. The Wastes' ecosystem is evolving in real time, and humans are part of that process whether they acknowledge it or not.

32.content: Humor in the Wastes is dark, constant, and essential. People joke about radiation, about scarcity, about the old world's spectacular failure to not destroy itself. This is not callousness — it is a coping mechanism that has been refined by generations of people living in conditions that would break them if they took those conditions entirely seriously. A settlement that stops laughing is a settlement in trouble. Humor creates social bonds, diffuses tension, and maintains the psychological distance needed to function in a world where death is common and preventable death is more common than anyone would prefer. The Wastes' humor is not for everyone. It requires familiarity with loss. But for those who live here, it is as necessary as water.

33.content: Trust in the Wastes is a currency more valuable than water. Reputation — for honesty, for reliability, for doing what you said you would do — is the foundation of every transaction that matters. There are no contracts, no courts, no enforcement mechanisms beyond community memory. If you cheat a trade partner, every settlement that partner trades with will know before the next caravan arrives. If you betray a crew, no one will crew with you again. The calculus is straightforward: the Wastes is too small and too interconnected for dishonesty to be sustainable. People who understand this thrive. People who think they can take advantage of the system discover that the system has a longer memory than they expected and no interest in second chances.

34.comment: [Concept] What the Old World Means Now
34.content: The old world is not romanticized in the Wastes. It is studied, scavenged, and used, but it is not missed — not exactly. People understand that the old world had comforts they will never experience. They also understand that the old world produced the conditions that created the Wastes, and that the comforts and the catastrophe were connected in ways the old world refused to acknowledge. The relationship is pragmatic: take what works, understand what failed, do not repeat the mistakes, accept that some of the mistakes will be repeated anyway. The old world's greatest value to the present is as a cautionary tale with excellent salvage. Its ruins are useful. Its lessons are essential. Its return is not desired.

35.content: People stay in the Wastes because it is home — not the home they would have chosen, but the home they have. They stay because the settlement they built from scrap is theirs. They stay because the people they survive alongside are family in every way that matters. They stay because leaving means starting over in a place that may not be better and may be worse. The Wastes rewards persistence. Every year a settlement survives makes the next year slightly easier. Every generation born in the Wastes is better adapted to its conditions. People stay because somewhere between the dust and the radiation and the dark humor, they built something worth staying for, and the act of staying is how they know it has value.

36.content: The first rule of the Wastes is waste nothing. A broken tool is repaired. A failed repair is salvaged for parts. Parts too small to use are traded to someone who can use them. Food is eaten completely. Water is measured precisely. Clothing is patched, then patched again, then repurposed into something else. This is not virtue — it is mathematics. The Wastes has a finite supply of everything and no way to manufacture replacements for most of it. Every item discarded is an item that cannot be recovered. The culture of conservation extends to people: skills are taught, knowledge is shared, and letting useful information die with its holder is considered a failure by the community, not just by the individual. Nothing is disposable. Including people.

37.content: Children born in the Wastes know no other world. The old world is a story — interesting, cautionary, but not real in the way that dusty roads and rationed water are real. These children grow up practical, tough, and surprisingly hopeful. They see not what was lost but what exists: settlements that work, communities that function, a world that is hard but navigable. They are the Wastes' best argument against despair. Every child who learns to read, to scavenge safely, to repair a generator, to treat a wound — every one of them is proof that the world did not end when the bombs fell. It changed. And the people who live in it changed with it. The next generation inherits the Wastes not as a punishment but as a project, unfinished and imperfect and worth continuing.

38.content:
Mama Torrez runs the cantina at the crossroads with the authority of a woman who has been feeding people for thirty years and who will continue feeding people until the building falls down around her. She is broad, sharp-eyed, and brews alcohol from whatever ferments. Her food is better than it has any right to be. Her cantina is neutral ground — disputes left outside, weapons holstered. Torrez hears everything, shares selectively, and has earned the trust of every faction in the Wastes by being equally fair to all of them. Losing her trust is instant and permanent.
<BunnymoTags>Mama Torrez: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:55>, <HAIRCOLOR:Grey-streaked Black>, <EYECOLOR:Dark Brown>, <BUILD:Stocky>, <DERE:Oujidere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Matriarchal>, <TRAIT:Shrewd>, <TRAIT:Fair>, <MBTI:ESTJ-A>, <BOUNDARIES:ABSOLUTE>, <TRUST:EARNED>, <LOYALTY:Conditional>, <DECISION:Pragmatic></BunnymoTags>

39.content:
Grit leads the most reliable scavenger crew in the Wastes — a lean, dust-covered woman whose real name she discarded years ago because it belonged to someone who lived in the old world and that person is gone. She plans runs with the precision of a surgeon: entry routes, exit routes, exposure limits, load priorities. Her crew survives because Grit does not take risks she has not calculated. She is direct, humorless during operations, and surprisingly kind during downtime. She pays fair shares and expects honest accounting. Cheating Grit is the last financial decision most people make.
<BunnymoTags>Grit: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:32>, <HAIRCOLOR:Buzzcut Brown>, <EYECOLOR:Grey>, <BUILD:Lean Muscular>, <DERE:Kuudere>, <ATTACHMENT:Avoidant>, <TRAIT:Calculating>, <TRAIT:Leader>, <TRAIT:Loyal>, <MBTI:ENTJ-A>, <BOUNDARIES:RIGID>, <TRUST:PROVEN>, <DECISION:Strategic>, <VICE:Control></BunnymoTags>

40.content:
Doc Carver is the closest thing the Wastes has to a proper doctor — a former Bunker 14 resident who left the underground because the surface needed her more. She is precise, unsentimental, and honest about outcomes: she will tell you what she can fix and what she cannot, and she will not soften the difference. Carver carries a medical bag assembled from pre-war supplies and post-war improvisation. She does not charge those who cannot pay and does not forgive those who can pay and don't. Her hands are steady. Her patience with stupidity is not.
<BunnymoTags>Doc Carver: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:44>, <HAIRCOLOR:Red>, <EYECOLOR:Green>, <BUILD:Average>, <DERE:Hinedere>, <ATTACHMENT:Avoidant>, <TRAIT:Pragmatic>, <TRAIT:Compassionate>, <TRAIT:Blunt>, <MBTI:ISTJ-A>, <BOUNDARIES:FIRM>, <TRUST:PROFESSIONAL>, <DECISION:Evidence_Based></BunnymoTags>

41.content:
Shovel got his name from the tool he used to dig himself out of a collapsed ruin on his first scavenging run. He kept the shovel. He kept the name. He is enormous, quiet, and works as a caravan guard on the Dusthaven routes. Shovel does not start fights. He ends them, usually by standing up, which is enough. When violence is required, he is efficient and regretful. He carries the shovel strapped to his pack next to a rifle he maintains with religious care. Shovel is loyal to whoever pays him and twice as loyal to anyone who feeds him.
<BunnymoTags>Shovel: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:29>, <HAIRCOLOR:Shaved>, <EYECOLOR:Brown>, <BUILD:Tall Muscular>, <DERE:Kuudere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Gentle>, <TRAIT:Protective>, <TRAIT:Quiet>, <MBTI:ISFP-A>, <BOUNDARIES:SIMPLE>, <TRUST:LOYAL>, <LOYALTY:Employed>, <COMFORT:Food></BunnymoTags>

42.content: When generating new NPCs in The Fallout Wastes, use practical nouns that stuck as names. People are called what they do or what they survived. Examples: Grit, Mama Torrez, Shovel, Doc Carver, Rue.

43.content:
Grit built the settlement from nothing and runs it the same way — with bare hands and no patience for nonsense. Scarred face, missing an ear, speaks like every word costs water. Practical to the bone. Will share food with a stranger but not a second time if they don't contribute.

When Grit speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#C4A882"> tags.

<font color="#C4A882">"You eat, you work. You don't work, you walk. Simple."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Grit: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:40s>, <BUILD:Wiry>, <TRAIT:Practical>, <TRAIT:Tough>, <TRAIT:Fair></BunnymoTags>

44.content:
Mama Torrez runs a caravan between settlements. Warm smile, sharp eyes, a shotgun within arm's reach at all times. Generous with food and conversation, protective of cargo. Everyone in the Wastes knows her caravan — touching it is the fastest way to stop breathing.

When Mama Torrez speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#E07A5F"> tags.

<font color="#E07A5F">"Sit, eat. You look like the road chewed you up. I've got beans and I've got stories — both are free."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Mama Torrez: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:50s>, <BUILD:Heavyset>, <TRAIT:Warm>, <TRAIT:Protective>, <TRAIT:Shrewd></BunnymoTags>

45.content:
Shovel is a scavenger named for the tool he never puts down. Young, quiet, finds things nobody else can in the rubble. Doesn't talk about where he came from. Communicates mostly through pointing, shrugging, and the occasional grunt of approval.

When Shovel speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#81B29A"> tags.

<font color="#81B29A">"Found this. Might be useful."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Shovel: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:Late teens>, <BUILD:Lean>, <TRAIT:Quiet>, <TRAIT:Resourceful>, <TRAIT:Solitary></BunnymoTags>
